findings.kidney_retrained_regression: kidney_lr_v3_hard_neg Top-1 dropped 86%→2% on synth cohort. Root cause: the two new discriminative features (serum_creatinine_mg_dl, LBXSUA_uric_acid_mg_dl) are NOT in the synthetic cohort lab_values dict. Both get imputed to training median (≈0 z-score = normal), stripping the model of its key anchors. On the NHANES holdout (real patients with real lab values), the model correctly achieves 80.8% Top-1.
findings.inflammation_retrained_top1_zero: hidden_inflammation_lr_v4_hard_neg Top-1 = 0% on synth cohort (vs 98% prod). Cause: removing class_weight=balanced reduced calibrated scores from ~0.65 to ~0.21 mean. The prediabetes model still over-fires (63.2% FP, mean ~0.58), outranking inflammation for every profile in this 3-model comparison. Top-3 = 100% — the signal is present but not dominant in a 3-model ranking. In the full 13-model production ranking, inflammation would not always rank behind prediabetes since other models have lower scores too.
findings.prediabetes_no_regression: prediabetes v3 maintains Top-1=100% on synth cohort. bmi_x_family_dm interaction is derivable from synthetic profile demographics (BMI) + questionnaire (family_dm).
findings.recommendation: 1. Kidney v3: Add serum_creatinine_mg_dl + uric_acid to cohort generator lab_values for future evals, so kidney-disease profiles carry these values. 2. Inflammation v4: Evaluate in full 13-model context (run_eval.py) rather than 3-model Top-1 — the 3-model comparison is unfair due to prediabetes over-firing. 3. Consider partial class_weight (e.g. {0:1, 1:3}) for inflammation to recover Top-1 without re-inflating the intercept.
